hard.next_step: 5.19.26 KK - chris and monica looking for stakeholder buyin; 5.4.26 KK - fu on proposal / next steps; 4.20.26 KK - fu on pricing to SWRI; 4.13.26 KK - submitted QIT
hard.ais_why: Scope work is ACH/banking validation and supplier onboarding, no Merlin/agentic cues from either side; next steps center on supplier master, risk and contract repository (functional framing, not AI-led); Qualified $51K deal with sparse AI excitement.
hard.competitor: Status quo (manual PDF-based supplier onboarding and phone-based ACH validation / do-nothing); one unnamed all-encompassing supplier-management vendor (final two)
hard.primary_competitor: Status quo / internal change-management inertia (no-decision risk)

north_star_verdict.headline: A well-received demo put Zycus in the buyer's final two, but the deal has gone quiet for 55 days, no economic buyer (CFO) is engaged, and the real blocker is the buyer's own change-management fear, not a rival.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 20 Mar 2026, Qualified since 24 Mar (104 days in stage, no progression). Last buyer touch 12 May 2026 (55 days ago); three rep follow-ups since (4 May, 19 May) drew no substantive buyer reply. Close date 19 Mar 2027 is 256 days out. The buyer's aspirational 60-day decision window (stated early May) has lapsed. To reach Vendor Selected by year-end 2026 (a realistic gate for a Q1-2027 close), the deal must move Qualified to Formal Evaluation within 30 days, which requires re-engagement and CFO/stakeholder alignment that has not occurred.
evidence: Last verified Salesforce activity 12 May 2026, 55 days ago (source: SF LastActivityDate)
Rep follow-ups documented 19 May ('chris and monica looking for stakeholder buyin') and 4 May ('fu on proposal / next steps'), no substantive buyer response (source: SF Next_Step__c)
Buyer put Zycus in the final two on the 2 Apr demo: 'I would say y'all are top two right now' and 'definitely beats what we're doing now' (source: Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026, Cody Ellerbee)
Economic buyer is the CFO by the buyer's own account but is unengaged: 'our biggest hurdle is our CFO accepting a third party validation software' (source: Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026, Cody Ellerbee)
Single-threaded through Cody Ellerbee (Manager, Evaluator), who has been silent 55 days (source: SF Contact Roles, SF LastActivityDate)
Amount cut from $120,000 to $51,150 on 15 Apr 2026, a defensive narrowing to the phase-1 ACH use case (source: SF Field History)

metrics.narrative: The buyer confirmed a concrete operational burden on the 30 Mar discovery and 2 Apr demo: they onboard 'just a little over 500 a year' suppliers manually (Monica Romero, 30 Mar) and each new supplier or bank-detail change triggers a manual phone-based ACH validation that the Zycus SE quantified as 8 to 9 hours per week, unchallenged by the buyer. Cody Ellerbee also named a fraud-risk metric: paying a wrong ACH account 'could be significantly high cost, way more than the 8 to 9 hours.' What is missing is a dollarized ROI or annual-savings figure the CFO could approve; Alexa Bradley explicitly steered away from a full business case ('Instead of doing a full blown business case, which I wouldn't always suggest for a full suite... we can just support you with the outcome percentages,' 2 Apr). Building that quantified value case is the open move.
sources: Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026 (Devika/Zycus: 8-9 hrs/week; Cody Ellerbee: ACH fraud cost)

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer is functionally identified but not named or engaged. Cody Ellerbee stated plainly on 2 Apr that 'our biggest hurdle is our CFO accepting a third party validation software... regardless of however many there are,' establishing the CFO as the true budget-and-acceptance gate, and separately noted his own boss reviews and passes a summary to 'executive management' (26 Mar email). No CFO is named in Salesforce Contact Roles and none has appeared on any call, so the person cannot be confirmed by name. Budget authority is further shaped by the nonprofit context Cody raised, weighing an ACH tool against funding core research. The move is to get Cody to name and broker an introduction to the CFO and executive-management sponsor before pricing can be defended.
sources: Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026 (Cody Ellerbee: CFO is the hurdle)
SF Task/email 26 Mar 2026 (Cody: boss passes summary to executive management)
SF Contact Roles: no EB named

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's criteria are clear from the two discovery/demo calls. First and foremost is automated ACH/banking validation to replace manual phone verification, which Cody called 'the main component.' Second is an all-encompassing, outward-facing self-service supplier portal that captures and scores data, which the buyer used to differentiate the field: 'we've only seen one other, like, all encompassing type where they're willing to have an outward facing portal... other ones are like, you can enter in the ACH information... and we'll tell you if it's good or bad' (Cody, 2 Apr). Third is Deltek Costpoint integration feasibility (API to their vendor database). Fourth, and decisive, is ease of adoption/change management for a PDF-based team. CUI/government-compliance handling appears in the rep's next-step notes but was not validated with the buyer on a call, so it is inferred.
sources: Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026 (Cody Ellerbee: all-encompassing portal, ACH, Deltek)

decision_process.narrative: Reading the call and email sequence, the process is bottom-up and informal: Cody Ellerbee (Manager, Services Purchasing) and Monica Romero (Purchasing) are running the evaluation, then packaging a summary email for Cody's boss to pass to executive management, with the CFO as the acceptance gate for third-party validation. Cody described this on 26 Mar ('I'd like to have yall included in my summary email she can hopefully pass along and executive management can kick it around') and the 19 May next-step ('chris and monica looking for stakeholder buyin') shows the internal buy-in step is still in progress and stalled. There is no formal procurement track, RFP, or committee yet. The buyer floated a ~60-day decision target for the banking-verification solution (early May) that has since lapsed with no confirmation.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are early and largely undiscussed. A commercial SaaS proposal was delivered 15 Apr 2026 with a scoping workshop recommended for the one-time implementation cost; no MSA, Order Form, SOW, legal review, or InfoSec track has begun. Two paper dependencies are visible: the buyer must separately procure the third-party banking-validation service (GIACT/GAIAC), which Devika confirmed is a direct buyer contract ('it would be your subscription with GAIAT'), adding a cost line Cody must factor; and CFO sign-off on accepting third-party validation is the gate. Implementation timeline was quoted at 2-3 months without integration, plus ~1-1.5 months with Deltek. Visibility will come from confirming the internal approval chain and the buyer's procurement steps for both Zycus and the validation vendor.
sources: SF Task/email 15 Apr 2026 (commercial SaaS proposal + scoping workshop)
Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026 (GIACT direct subscription; 2-3 month implementation)

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is confirmed and owned by Cody Ellerbee and Monica Romero in Purchasing. Today a new-vendor request triggers a PDF packet, one admin keys company data, a second person manually phone-verifies banking details, and re-activations after 1-2 dormant years repeat the whole cycle (Monica, 30 Mar). Cody framed the burden and the fear vividly on 2 Apr: 'we operate off PDFs... it's scary and overwhelming... getting the buy in and getting people to sign off on it.' The confirmed pains are labor (8-9 hrs/week, ~500 vendors/yr), ACH fraud exposure from manual verification, and poor vendor-data quality from handwritten forms. The inferred pain is compliance/audit strain (diversity checks, spreadsheet audit reports) which the buyer acknowledged as lower priority.

champion.narrative: Cody Ellerbee (Manager, Services Purchasing, Evaluator, primary contact) is the developing champion, and the strength is weak and cooling. He drove the evaluation, articulated pain and criteria, praised Zycus as 'top two,' and requested the proposal, which is genuine advocacy. What is missing: he has not introduced Zycus to the CFO or executive management he himself named as the gate, and he has been silent for 55 days despite three rep follow-ups (last buyer touch 12 May). Monica Romero (Administrative Coordinator/Purchasing Specialist) is a supportive influencer but holds no decision authority. The 19 May next-step shows Cody and Monica seeking internal buy-in but with no result. To convert Cody from evaluator to true champion he must broker the CFO/executive introduction, not just relay a summary email.
sources: SF Contact Roles (Cody Ellerbee - Evaluator - Primary; Monica Romero)
SF Next_Step__c 19 May 2026; SF LastActivityDate 12 May 2026

competition.narrative: The dominant competitor is the status quo / do-nothing: a PDF-and-phone manual process the team has run 'for so long,' and the buyer's own admitted fear of adoption is the biggest force keeping them there ('it's scary and overwhelming'). This is a no-decision risk, not a loss to a rival. There is exactly one named-by-category software alternative: an unnamed all-encompassing supplier-management vendor with an outward-facing portal that put Zycus in a final two ('I would say y'all are top two right now,' Cody, 2 Apr); other vendors the buyer saw only offer partial ACH-entry tooling and are effectively out. Third-party validation providers (GIACT/GAIAC, and a JP Morgan reference) are Zycus partners in the workflow, not competitors. We win by de-risking change management (Alexa's 'lead with adoption, then technology' reframe), proving the all-encompassing portal + automated bank validation the partial vendors lack, and landing a CFO-grade ROI.
sources: Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026 (Cody Ellerbee: 'top two', 'only seen one other all encompassing type')

deal_movement.summary: Momentum peaked at the 2 Apr demo (Zycus named final two) and the 15 Apr proposal, then decayed: the amount was halved the same day the proposal went out, and the deal has been buyer-silent for 55 days while rep follow-ups and an internal buy-in push (19 May) produced no reply.

items.change: Buyer silent 55 days; last buyer touch was Monica Romero acknowledging rep follow-up ('continuing to try to nail down key stakeholders')
Rep next-step logged internal buy-in attempt in progress ('chris and monica looking for stakeholder buyin'), no buyer response since
Amount cut from $120,000 to $51,150, narrowing to the phase-1 ACH/banking-validation use case
Commercial SaaS proposal delivered with a recommended scoping workshop for implementation cost
Focused ACH/onboarding demo delivered; buyer placed Zycus in the final two and said it 'beats what we're doing now'
Discovery confirmed ~500 vendors/yr onboarded manually and 8-9 hrs/week of manual ACH validation

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is the buyer's own status-quo and change-management inertia, a no-decision risk they named repeatedly on 2 Apr ('scary and overwhelming... getting the buy in'). The only live software rival is one unnamed all-encompassing supplier-management vendor that shares the final two with Zycus; partial ACH-only tools the buyer saw are effectively out. Third-party bank-validation providers (GIACT, JP Morgan) are workflow partners, not competitors, and should not be read as rivals.

competitors.how_we_win: Prove the end-to-end self-service portal plus automated GIACT bank validation and Deltek integration the partial tools cannot match; land a CFO-grade ROI.

competitors.quote: other ones are like, well, you can enter in the ACH information that you obtained... and then we'll tell you if it's good or bad
competitors.how_we_win: Contrast scope: outward-facing supplier self-service and lifecycle vs a point ACH checker.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Zycus fits the buyer's stated must-haves on capability (all-encompassing portal, automated bank validation) but is exposed on the two things that actually decide this deal: internal adoption/change management and CFO acceptance of third-party validation. Deltek integration feasibility is unproven.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers matter now: break the single-thread to the CFO, lead with a change-management/adoption narrative, and put a dollarized ROI in front of the budget owner.
flags: Multi-thread to the CFO and executive-management sponsor Cody named as the acceptance gate; single-thread through one cooling evaluator is the top structural risk.
Lead the next buyer conversation with adoption and change-management (Alexa's Flintstones-to-Jetsons reframe), then the technology, to overcome the buyer's stated fear.
Dollarize the ROI (8-9 hrs/week plus ACH fraud-loss avoidance) against nonprofit budget pressure so Cody can defend the spend to the CFO.

champion_strength.summary: Cody Ellerbee is a developing champion whose strength is weak and at risk. He drove discovery, praised Zycus as 'top two,' and pulled the proposal, but he has not introduced the CFO/executive sponsor he named as the gate and has gone silent for 55 days after three rep follow-ups. Until he brokers the internal introduction, advocacy is stalling and the deal is exposed to no-decision.
champion_strength.source: SF Contact Roles; Avoma demo 2 Apr 2026; SF Next_Step__c 19 May 2026; SF LastActivityDate 12 May 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus showed real AI in the demo (autonomous document-extraction agent for W-9s, AI-assisted data capture, intake orchestration agent in Teams, AI redlining), but the buyer is functionally motivated by ACH automation and change-management, and never engaged the AI story. AI is a supporting proof point here, not the buying lever, so heavier agentic positioning would not move this deal.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 4.0 Moderate matches the evidence: AI capabilities exist and were demoed but the buyer's priorities are functional (bank validation, onboarding, adoption), with no agentic pull from Cody or Monica across three calls.

items.detail: Buyer's fear of adoption for a PDF-based team is the primary threat and a no-decision risk: 'it's scary and overwhelming... getting the buy in and getting people to sign off.'
CFO named as the acceptance gate for third-party validation but never engaged; no executive sponsor mapped.
Nonprofit budget pressure; Cody weighed the ACH tool against funding core research, and GIACT is a separate buyer-borne cost line.
Deltek Costpoint API integration feasibility unvalidated; without it, onboarded records must be re-keyed manually into Costpoint.
The buyer's aspirational 60-day decision window has lapsed and the deal has sat 104 days in Qualified with no progression.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling to dark: 55 days of buyer silence after a strong 2 Apr demo, three unanswered rep follow-ups, and no CFO/stakeholder progression.

win_position.text: Zycus is one of the buyer's final two after the 2 Apr demo: Cody Ellerbee said we are 'top two right now' and 'definitely beats what we're doing now,' then pulled a proposal (delivered 15 Apr).
No economic buyer is engaged; Cody named the CFO as the real gate ('our biggest hurdle is our CFO accepting a third party validation software,' 2 Apr) but the CFO has never been contacted.

customer_commitment.text: The buyer ran real discovery and a focused demo, disclosed ~500 vendors/yr onboarded manually and 8-9 hrs/week of ACH validation, and requested a proposal.
Commitment is soft: no next meeting booked, no CFO introduction, and the internal buy-in push ('chris and monica looking for stakeholder buyin,' 19 May) has produced no movement.

deal_risk.text: Change-management inertia is the primary threat: 'it's scary and overwhelming... getting the buy in and getting people to sign off' (Cody, 2 Apr) points to a do-nothing/no-decision risk, not a loss to a rival.
Scope was halved from $120k to $51k on 15 Apr, a defensive narrowing to a single phase-1 ACH use case.
Nonprofit budget pressure plus an unproven CFO acceptance of third-party validation leaves the spend undefended.

scope_change.from: $120,000 (full-suite framing across iContract, iSupplier, iRisk, iCompliance)
scope_change.to: $51,150 (phase-1 iSupplier ACH/banking validation and supplier onboarding)
scope_change.detail: Amount cut on 15 Apr 2026 (SF Field History). The buyer explicitly deferred contract lifecycle and risk modules to a phase-2 expansion and focused first on the ACH/banking-validation use case, a defensive, phased posture consistent with nonprofit budget pressure and change-management caution.

critical_signals.text: The 2 Apr demo landed well: Cody Ellerbee put Zycus in the final two and said it beats the current manual process, but committed only to requesting a proposal.
The real contest is the buyer's own status-quo and change-management fear, a no-decision risk, not a named rival; one unnamed all-encompassing vendor shares the final two.
The CFO surfaced as the true approval gate for accepting third-party validation but has never been engaged and is not named in the record.
A $51k proposal (halved from $120k) went to a budget-constrained nonprofit on 15 Apr with no ROI case landed and no buyer feedback in 55 days.
Deltek Costpoint integration feasibility is the buyer's open technical question and is still unvalidated.
